title: A/B Testing a Call to Action

yaml.title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
yaml.subtitle: Simulating Key Ideas from Classical Frequentist Statistics
yaml.author: Amelia Li

markdown.markdown:
```{r, include=FALSE}
library(tidyverse)
library(broom)
library(gt)
set.seed(12345)
```



If you run a website, even a small wording change on a landing page can affect how many users sign up. Suppose my site includes a newsletter signup box, and I want to compare two possible calls to action. CTA A says, ‚ÄúSign up for our newsletter here!‚ÄĚ, while CTA B says, ‚ÄúStay up to date by signing up!‚ÄĚ These two messages are similar, but they may not perform the same way.

To learn which version works better, I can run an A/B test. In an A/B test, visitors are randomly assigned to one of two groups. One group sees CTA A and the other sees CTA B. I then compare the signup rates across the two groups. Because assignment is random, this gives me a clean way to estimate whether one CTA is more effective than the other.

## The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem

Each visitor either signs up or does not sign up, so each outcome can be modeled as a Bernoulli random variable. Let $\pi$ denote the probability of signup.

Since the CTA a visitor sees may affect that probability, I allow the Bernoulli parameter to differ across the two groups. Let $\pi_A$ be the probability that a user signs up after seeing CTA A, and let $\pi_B$ be the probability after seeing CTA B.

The parameter of interest is:

$$
\theta = \pi_A - \pi_B
$$
A natural estimator is: 

$$
\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B
$$

Since the data are binary, this is simply the difference in observed signup rates.

## Simulating Data
In practice, we do not know the true values of $\pi_A$ and $\pi_B$. For this exercise, we set them manually to study how the estimator behaves.

Suppose:
ŌÄA‚Äč=0.22,ŌÄB‚Äč=0.18,őł=0.04


```{r}
n_A <- 1000
n_B <- 1000

pi_A <- 0.22
pi_B <- 0.18
theta_true <- pi_A - pi_B

x_A <- rbinom(n_A, 1, pi_A)
x_B <- rbinom(n_B, 1, pi_B)

theta_hat <- mean(x_A) - mean(x_B)

theta_hat
```


## The Law of Large Numbers
The Law of Large Numbers tells us that sample averages converge to population averages as the sample size grows. This gives us confidence that $\hat\theta$ will be close to $\theta$ in large samples.


```{r}
diff_vec <- x_A - x_B
cum_avg <- cumsum(diff_vec) / seq_along(diff_vec)

lln_df <- tibble(n = 1:1000, cum_avg = cum_avg)
```

```{r}
ggplot(lln_df, aes(n, cum_avg)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = theta_true, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(title = "Convergence of the Estimated Effect",
       x = "Observations",
       y = "Cumulative Mean") +
  theme_minimal()
```


The estimate is noisy at first but stabilizes as sample size increases, approaching the true value.

## Bootstrap Standard Errors

To measure uncertainty, we use the bootstrap.


```{r}
B <- 1000

boot_thetas <- replicate(B, {
  mean(sample(x_A, replace = TRUE)) -
    mean(sample(x_B, replace = TRUE))
})

boot_se <- sd(boot_thetas)

pihat_A <- mean(x_A)
pihat_B <- mean(x_B)

analytical_se <- sqrt(
  pihat_A * (1 - pihat_A) / n_A +
  pihat_B * (1 - pihat_B) / n_B
)

tibble(
  bootstrap_se = boot_se,
  analytical_se = analytical_se
)
```


The two standard errors are very similar, indicating consistency between methods.


```{r}
ci <- theta_hat + c(-1.96, 1.96) * boot_se
ci
```

This gives a 95% confidence interval for $\theta$.

## The Central Limit Theorem

The CLT states that the sampling distribution of $\hat\theta$ becomes approximately Normal as sample size increases.

```{r}
sample_sizes <- c(25, 50, 100, 500)

clt_df <- map_dfr(sample_sizes, function(n) {
  tibble(
    n = factor(n),
    theta_hat = replicate(1000,
      mean(rbinom(n,1,pi_A)) - mean(rbinom(n,1,pi_B))
    )
  )
})
```

```{r, echo=FALSE}
ggplot(clt_df, aes(x = theta_hat)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, color = "white") +
  facet_wrap(~n, ncol = 2, scales = "fixed") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = theta_true, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    title = "Sampling Distribution of the Difference in Means",
    x = expression(hat(theta)),
    y = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```


As sample size increases, the distribution becomes smoother and more bell-shaped.

## Hypothesis Testing

We test: 

$$
H_0: \theta = 0
\qquad
H_1: \theta \neq 0
$$

```{r}
z_stat <- theta_hat / analytical_se
p_value <- 2 * (1 - pnorm(abs(z_stat)))

tibble(z_stat, p_value)
```

A small p-value suggests evidence against the null hypothesis. In this simulated sample, the p-value is about 0.079, so I would not reject the null hypothesis at the 5% level. Although the estimated effect is positive, the evidence is not strong enough in this particular sample to rule out a zero difference.

## The T-Test as a Regression

We can rewrite the test as: Yi‚Äč=ő≤0‚Äč+ő≤1‚ÄčDi‚Äč+őĶi‚Äč

```{r}
df <- tibble(
  y = c(x_A, x_B),
  D = c(rep(1,n_A), rep(0,n_B))
)

reg_fit <- lm(y ~ D, data = df)
tidy(reg_fit) |>
  select(term, estimate, std.error, statistic, p.value) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), round, 4)) |>
  gt()
```

The coefficient on $D$ equals $\hat\theta$.The regression estimate matches the difference in sample means, and the p-value is also very similar to the one from the two-sample z-test. This illustrates the equivalence between the two approaches.

## The Problem with Peeking

Repeated testing inflates false positives.

```{r}
one_run <- function() {
  xA <- rbinom(1000,1,0.2)
  xB <- rbinom(1000,1,0.2)

  any(sapply(seq(100,1000,100), function(n){
    pA <- mean(xA[1:n])
    pB <- mean(xB[1:n])
    se <- sqrt(pA*(1-pA)/n + pB*(1-pB)/n)
    z <- (pA - pB)/se
    2*(1-pnorm(abs(z))) < 0.05
  }))
}

mean(replicate(10000, one_run()))
```

The false positive rate exceeds 5%, showing that peeking leads to misleading conclusions.In the simulation, the false positive rate under repeated peeking is about 0.196, which is far above the nominal 0.05 level for a single test. This shows that repeatedly checking the data can dramatically inflate Type I error.

## Conclusion

This simulation illustrates key ideas in frequentist statistics: consistency, uncertainty estimation, asymptotic normality, and hypothesis testing. It also highlights practical pitfalls like peeking, which can invalidate results if not handled properly.

library(broom)
